title: Modern Frontend Track
content: Frontend roles in 2026 expect strong React or Vue fundamentals, TypeScript, component architecture, and accessibility basics. Trending tools: Next.js App Router, server components, Tailwind, Vite, Zustand or Jotai for state. Strong portfolios show at least one performance-optimized, responsive, deployed app rather than tutorial clones. Good project ideas: a real-time collaborative tool, a design-system/component library published to npm, or a data-heavy dashboard with virtualization.

title: Backend & API Design Track
content: Backend roles value REST/GraphQL API design, database modeling (SQL and NoSQL), authentication/authorization, caching, and basic system design (load balancing, queues, rate limiting). Trending: Node.js with TypeScript, Go, or Python/FastAPI, plus Redis and PostgreSQL. Good project ideas: a multi-tenant SaaS backend with rate limiting and background jobs, a URL shortener with analytics, or a chat backend using websockets and message queues.

title: Applied ML Track
content: Data science and ML roles expect Python, pandas, scikit-learn, and a portfolio showing the full pipeline: data cleaning, feature engineering, model evaluation, and clear communication of results. Deep learning roles add PyTorch or TensorFlow. Trending: LLM fine-tuning, evaluation harnesses, and MLOps basics (experiment tracking, model versioning). Good project ideas: an end-to-end Kaggle-style project with a written report, a churn or fraud prediction model deployed as an API, or a small fine-tuned model with a documented evaluation.

title: LLM & Generative AI Applications Track
content: LLM application engineering values prompt engineering, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), agentic workflows, and API integration (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic). Employers look for projects that go beyond a chatbot wrapper: multi-step agents, tool use, structured output, and evaluation of hallucination or grounding quality. Good project ideas: a RAG system over a domain-specific corpus with citation, a multi-agent workflow that plans and executes tasks, or a fine-tuned or prompt-optimized assistant with measured accuracy improvements.

title: Cloud & DevOps Track
content: DevOps and cloud roles expect Docker, CI/CD pipelines, and one major cloud provider (AWS, GCP, or Azure). Trending: Kubernetes basics, infrastructure as code (Terraform), and observability (logging, metrics, tracing). Good project ideas: a CI/CD pipeline that auto-deploys a personal project with rollback, a small Kubernetes cluster running a multi-service app, or an infrastructure-as-code template for a common stack.

title: Mobile App Development Track
content: Mobile roles expect either native (Kotlin/Swift) or cross-platform (React Native, Flutter) proficiency, along with state management, offline support, and app store deployment experience. Good project ideas: an offline-first app with local sync, a polished app published to a store (even informally via TestFlight/APK), or a mobile app that integrates device sensors or camera in a non-trivial way.

title: Security & AppSec Track
content: Security roles expect understanding of common vulnerability classes (OWASP Top 10), secure coding practice, and some hands-on CTF or bug bounty experience. Trending: cloud security, container security, and basic threat modeling. Good project ideas: a documented vulnerability writeup from a CTF, a small static analysis or dependency-scanning tool, or a hardened deployment of a common app with a security checklist.'

title: Algorithms & Problem Solving Track
content: Strong DSA fundamentals (arrays, trees, graphs, DP, greedy, number theory) remain the baseline filter for most SDE interviews. Competitive programming ratings (Codeforces, CodeChef, LeetCode) are a credible, checkable signal recruiters scan for. Good additions: a consistent contest history, a few well-explained editorial-style writeups of hard problems, or a small library of reusable algorithm implementations.

title: Full-Stack Product Track
content: Full-stack roles value the ability to ship a complete product: frontend, backend, database, auth, and deployment. Recruiters look for at least one project with real users or a real deployment link, not just source code. Good project ideas: a small SaaS tool solving a specific personal problem, an open-source contribution to an existing full-stack repo, or a polished clone with one genuinely original feature.

title: Embedded & IoT Track
content: Embedded roles expect C/C++, microcontroller experience (ESP32, STM32, Arduino), and understanding of protocols like I2C, SPI, UART, and MQTT. Trending: edge AI on microcontrollers (TinyML) and IoT security. Good project ideas: a sensor network with a cloud dashboard, an edge-AI project running a small model on-device, or a home-automation system with a custom PCB or well-documented wiring.

title: Game Dev Track
content: Game development roles expect a shipped or demo-able game, familiarity with an engine (Unity or Unreal or a custom engine), and understanding of game loops, physics, and optimization. Good project ideas: a small polished game with real playtesting feedback incorporated, a custom shader or rendering technique writeup, or a multiplayer game with basic netcode.

title: Blockchain Track
content: Blockchain roles expect Solidity or Rust smart contract experience, understanding of gas optimization, and security awareness (reentrancy, overflow, access control). Good project ideas: an audited or self-audited smart contract with tests, a small DeFi primitive built from scratch, or a contribution to an open-source protocol.

title: Quality Engineering Track
content: QA and test engineering roles expect automated testing frameworks (Playwright, Cypress, Selenium), understanding of test pyramids, and CI integration. Good project ideas: an end-to-end test suite for an existing open-source app, a custom testing utility or linter, or a documented bug-hunting case study.

title: Data Analytics Track
content: Analytics roles expect SQL fluency, dashboarding tools (Tableau, Power BI, or a custom dashboard with Python/JS), and clear storytelling with data. Good project ideas: a dashboard built on a real public dataset with actionable insights, an A/B test analysis writeup, or a data pipeline that automates a recurring report.

title: Low-Level & Systems Track
content: Systems roles expect C/C++/Rust, understanding of memory management, concurrency, and OS fundamentals. Good project ideas: a small database or key-value store built from scratch, a custom memory allocator with benchmarks, or contributions to a systems-level open-source project.

title: Product-Minded Engineering
content: Engineers who can articulate the 'why' behind features stand out in interviews. This shows up as documenting user problems solved by a project, including before/after metrics or usage numbers, and writing concise READMEs that explain tradeoffs. Good additions to any resume: a short case-study style writeup for one flagship project explaining the problem, approach, and outcome.

title: Open Source Track
content: Merged pull requests to real open-source projects are a strong, verifiable signal of collaboration skill and code quality, often weighted more than solo projects. Good approach: start with documentation or test fixes to learn a codebase, then move to small feature or bug-fix PRs in a project relevant to the target role.

title: Resume Writing Best Practices
content: Strong resumes lead each bullet with an action verb and a measurable outcome (percentage, time saved, users reached) rather than a list of duties. Project sections should name the concrete problem solved and the stack used, with a live link when possible. One page is standard for students and early-career candidates; keep formatting simple and ATS-friendly (no tables or images for text content).
